Beyond distillation's potential uses in privacy, the attacks on distillation have implications for leaked private information during learning-based model extraction attacks.
Model extraction attacks often resemble model distillation, where an adversary reproduces the functionality of a target model into the weights of a local model.
In model extraction, an adversary uses API access to a target model to reproduce its functionality into the weights of a local model.
The target model in model extraction is analogous to the teacher model in distillation.
The local model in model extraction is analogous to the student in distillation.
Attacks in the Private Teacher threat model allow an adversary to inspect their local model to perform membership inference attacks on the target model's training data.

The Private Student threat model has implications for a defensive setting in model extraction, where the target model's owner wants to link queries made to the target model with a model they believe has been extracted from their model.

When introducing knowledge distillation, a modification known as temperature scaling was proposed by Hinton, Vinyals, Dean, et al., which makes two simple changes to distillation.
Temperature scaling involves introducing a temperature hyperparameter H, which when increased, rescales the logits and flattens the resulting probability distribution of student queries.
Temperature scaling also involves rescaling gradients by a 1/H 2 factor, which can improve performance by modifying this parameter.
